# HAIR

> HAIR (Home Assistant Infrared Registry) is a Home Assistant custom integration
> (HACS-compatible) that provides a full admin UI for infrared device management.
> Built on top of HA's native infrared platform (introduced in HA 2026.4), HAIR
> handles capturing, fingerprinting, deduplicating, organizing, and transmitting
> IR commands through a single sidebar panel at /hair. No YAML, no manual entity
> definitions, no automations required for basic IR device control.

HAIR sits between the user and HA's IR platform. It does not replace IR hardware
integrations (Broadlink, ESPHome, etc.). It complements them by providing the admin
layer those integrations lack -- analogous to how Z-Wave JS UI manages Z-Wave
devices on top of the Z-Wave JS integration.

Key components:

- HAIR Sniffer: a passive listener that captures every IR transmission seen by
  configured receivers, fingerprints each signal using short/long pulse-duration
  analysis, deduplicates repeated presses, filters out repeat frames (sent when
  buttons are held), and groups signals by source remote based on carrier frequency
  and preamble analysis. All in real time. Any signal can be given an alias (a
  user nickname that replaces the diamond pattern in the UI); an alias is a label
  on the signal, not a command name. Remotes, and the signals within a remote, can
  be drag-reordered; the order is persisted (it replaced the old hit-count sort),
  and a newly seen remote or signal surfaces at the top until the user moves it.
  A signal's raw Pronto can be edited in place from a single Pronto editor (shared
  by the Sniffer, the Clipper, and device commands); a bound trigger re-points
  automatically when an edit changes the S/L fingerprint, and a carrier reading off
  the consumer standards can be snapped to the nearest one (30, 33, 36, 38, 40, or
  56 kHz).
- Clipper: a manual-entry surface (a third panel tab) for building virtual remotes by
  pasting Pronto hex codes, for cases where a live signal cannot be sniffed. Each
  pasted code is validated (hex shape, four-digit words, a learned-code 0000 header,
  burst-pair length math, and a carrier-frequency sanity warning) and then behaves
  identically to a sniffed signal: test, trigger, assign, or promote. Pasted signals
  share the same catalog as sniffed signals, distinguished by a source field
  (sniffed, manual, or plucked). Manual and plucked remotes are never auto-evicted. Pasting an identical
  code already on a remote is rejected, but genuinely different codes that share an
  S/L fingerprint are kept as separate signals via a byte-level tiebreaker. Remotes
  and their signals are drag-reorderable like the Sniffer's. The Create Remote
  dialog can also pick a known manufacturer and model from the device codebooks in
  the installed Home Assistant infrared library and materialize a remote pre-filled
  with one signal per button; it is a shortcut for the supported devices, not a
  universal lookup.
- Plucker: a vendor-code import surface (a capture tab parallel to the Sniffer and
  Clipper) that pulls IR codes already learned into a vendor blaster into HAIR as
  native signals, without re-learning each code at a receiver. HAIR registers a
  no-transmit observer emitter on HA's infrared platform (the HAIR Tweezer, an
  InfraredEmitterEntity), asks the vendor integration to replay a stored code by
  name through that emitter, and captures the resulting Command before it becomes
  physical IR. No IR is broadcast during a pluck. It works with any integration
  that can replay a stored code by name through a caller-chosen emitter; Tuya Local
  (send_learned_ir_command) is the first. New vendors are added as a single YAML
  file in custom_components/hair/pluckable/ with no HAIR code changes, validated
  against a versioned schema. The Plucker tab and a Blasters (Pluckable) section on
  the Devices page appear only when a compatible blaster is configured. Requires HA
  2026.6+, where the infrared platform exports InfraredEmitterEntity. Plucked
  signals share the same catalog as sniffed and clipped ones, distinguished by the
  source field.
- Device Manager: lets users create profiles for IR-controlled devices (TVs, ACs,
  fans, lights, switches, screens, generic) with assigned IR emitters and learned
  commands. Assigning a sniffed or pasted signal copies it into the device as a
  command and leaves the source signal in the catalog, so one signal can be assigned
  to multiple devices or as multiple commands. Only delete, dismiss, and clear-all
  remove a signal. A device command can be renamed (its action mappings follow the
  new name) and given a send count so HAIR transmits the whole command more than once
  per press for a device that needs a repeat. The editor edits the command copy, not
  the catalog signal it came from.
- Entity Factory: automatically creates native HA entities based on device type --
  media_player, climate, fan, light, switch, cover, plus a universal remote entity
  for raw Pronto hex sending and a button entity for every learned command.
- Action Mapping: explicitly binds learned IR commands to HA entity features via a
  popover UI. A media_player only exposes volume control if commands are mapped to
  the volume actions, keeping entities clean and avoiding features the remote does
  not support.
- Trigger Manager: turns received IR signals into native HA event entities that
  fire hair_ir_command_received events. Any HA automation can consume them. A
  configurable min-hits threshold prevents accidental triggers from a single
  misfire. To the best of the project's knowledge, HAIR is the only HA integration
  turning received IR into automation triggers as of HA 2026.6.
  Location-aware (v0.5.7): the fire payload also carries receiver_entity_id plus
  receiver_area_id and receiver_area_name, resolved from the entity/device/area
  registry chain at fire time (so area renames and device moves need no migration).
  A trigger can carry a receiver_entity_ids scope list (empty = any receiver,
  backward compatible); scoped triggers fire only when a listed receiver observes
  the signal, and legacy ESPHome-bridge captures (receiver_entity_id=None) match
  only unscoped triggers. Multiple triggers per signal fingerprint are legal (one
  per room). A single press observed by several receivers within a 60ms window
  fires each matching trigger once (per-(trigger, fingerprint) dedup) and counts as
  one hit toward min_hits.

Signal fingerprinting details:
- Uses S/L (short/long) pulse-duration classification. Each pulse is classified as
  short or long, producing a pattern that identifies the signal regardless of minor
  timing jitter between presses. Protocols whose long pulse falls just below the S/L
  threshold (such as the Panasonic and TCL families) are separated by an additional
  byte-level tiebreaker so distinct commands are not merged.
- Patterns are rendered as two-tone diamond sequences in the UI for visual ID.
- Covers all major consumer IR protocols: NEC, Samsung, JVC, LG, Sony, RC-5, RC-6.
- Source-remote grouping uses carrier frequency and preamble fingerprinting, so the
  Sniffer can identify which remote sent a signal without decoding the protocol.
- When a captured signal can be read as a known protocol (NEC today), HAIR also
  stores the decoded identity alongside the raw timings as a cache layer. Raw
  timings stay the source of truth; the decoded form gives stronger matching and
  lets transmit re-encode clean timings instead of replaying captured ones.
- The decoded form drives every transmit path consistently: device commands, the
  catalog-signal Test button (Sniffer, Clipper, Plucker), and the guided Capture
  save all prefer the re-encoded clean timings when a decoded identity is present.
  Assigning a signal and saving a captured command carry that identity onto the new
  command at creation time, not just at the next load.
- Two user-tunable TX knobs, editable on catalog signals, device commands, and
  the assign dialog: Ditto count (the NEC repeat-frame count appended after the
  main frame, for receivers that need a held-key pattern) and Send times
  (whole-frame retransmissions). The catalog-signal Test path honors both,
  matching device TX. The Ditto count input and chip honor the per-command
  NEC/PRONTO toggle and the signal's decoded state: when a command is set to
  PRONTO transmit (tx_force_raw) or a signal has no decoded protocol, the chip
  and the matching editor / assign-dialog input all hide, since dittos do not
  fire on the raw TX path.
- At capture, NEC dittos that follow a main frame are observed (single-anchor
  attribution with a 1.0s window and a 250ms inter-frame gate, max-merged) and
  surfaced in the signal editor as a read-only "Observed at capture" hint. The
  observation does not auto-set the TX Ditto count; the user acts on it.

Supported capture providers (auto-discovered):
- Native InfraredReceiverEntity instances (HA 2026.6+, hardware-agnostic)
- ESPHome devices with the remote_receiver component (legacy bridge)
- Broadlink RM series devices

Supported transmit hardware: any IR emitter exposed to HA's infrared platform,
including ESPHome IR LEDs, Tuya Local IR blasters, Broadlink RM series, and
SMLIGHT SLZB devices.

Supported device types and resulting HA entities:
- Media Player -> media_player (power, volume, mute, source, channels, navigation, transport)
- AC -> climate (HVAC modes, temperature presets, fan modes)
- Fan -> fan (power, speed stepping, oscillate)
- Light -> light (on/off, brightness stepping)
- Switch -> switch (on/off)
- Screen -> cover (open, close, stop)
- Other -> remote (generic IR command sender)

Every device also gets a remote entity for raw Pronto hex codes and a button entity
per learned command for direct dashboard or automation access.

Requirements:
- Home Assistant 2026.4 or later (introduces the native infrared platform that HAIR
  depends on)
- Python 3.12+
- At least one IR transmitter or receiver supported by HA's infrared platform

What HAIR is NOT:
- Not a replacement for the Broadlink or ESPHome integrations -- HAIR complements
  them. The underlying IR hardware integrations are still required.
- Not a replacement for HA's infrared platform -- HAIR is an admin layer on top of
  the platform.
- Not compatible with Home Assistant versions earlier than 2026.4 (the infrared
  platform did not exist before then).
- Not a YAML-based integration -- all device profiles and command assignments are
  managed through the sidebar UI.
- Not a built-in Home Assistant integration -- must be installed via HACS as a
  custom repository.

Receiver architecture:
- On HA 2026.6+, HAIR uses the native InfraredReceiverEntity API
  (infrared.async_subscribe_receiver) for hardware-agnostic signal capture.
- On HA 2026.4-2026.5, HAIR falls back to the legacy ESPHome event bus bridge
  (esphome.remote_received events) automatically.
- Both paths convert signals to Pronto hex at the entry point for consistent
  fingerprinting.

## Repositories

- [HAIR (this repo)](https://github.com/DAB-LABS/HAIR): Python HACS integration
  (custom_components/hair/), TypeScript frontend panel served at /hair, HACS
  metadata (hacs.json), and project documentation.

## Documentation

- [README](https://github.com/DAB-LABS/HAIR/blob/main/README.md): Full feature
  overview, why-HAIR rationale, entity platform reference, Devices tab and Sniffer
  tab walkthroughs, installation and setup, and architecture diagram.
- [info.md](https://github.com/DAB-LABS/HAIR/blob/main/info.md): HACS short-form
  summary shown in the HACS UI during install.
- [Making your integration pluckable](https://github.com/DAB-LABS/HAIR/blob/main/docs/making-your-integration-pluckable.md):
  guide for HA integration authors on exposing the cross-emitter replay service the
  Plucker needs. The registry file format is documented in
  custom_components/hair/pluckable/README.md.
- [CHANGELOG](https://github.com/DAB-LABS/HAIR/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md): Release
  history.
- [CONTRIBUTING](https://github.com/DAB-LABS/HAIR/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md):
  Contribution guidelines, including the requirement that llms.txt be updated
  alongside README changes (per DAB-LABS LLMDO release protocol).

## Hardware configurations

- [esphome/](https://github.com/DAB-LABS/HAIR/tree/main/esphome):
  Curated, HAIR-tested ESPHome configurations for common IR hardware. Each
  config carries a machine-readable header (device, GPIO map, tested versions,
  contributor) for easy discovery. Two-tier convention per device: minimal
  (just the IR pieces) and full (preserves device-specific features like touch
  pads and status LEDs).

## Releases

- [Releases](https://github.com/DAB-LABS/HAIR/releases): Versioned releases. HACS
  installs from tagged releases.

## Optional

- [DAB-LABS](https://github.com/DAB-LABS): Other open-source Home Assistant and
  homelab projects by the same author, including SMART Sniffer (disk health
  monitoring) and esphome-ld2411s (mmWave presence sensor component).
